timeline_link.text: See on Timeline — Call of Jeremiah

sections.header: Verses 1–12 — "The Pen of the Scribes Has Handled It Falsely"

heb.paragraph: The scribes wield "the lying pen" (ʿēṭ šeqer, v.8) — a devastating charge that the custodians of Torah have distorted the very text they were entrusted to preserve. Šeqer pervades chapters 5–9 as the defining sin: false prophets (5:31), false worship (7:4), false texts (8:8), false peace (8:11). The entire knowledge-producing apparatus of Judean society traffics in deception.

God asks: "How can you say, 'We are wise (ḥākāmîm), for we have the law of the LORD'?" (v.8). The claim to wisdom through Torah possession is annulled when the Torah itself has been falsified. Wisdom without honest interpretation is worse than ignorance — it creates confident fools.

refs.note: The "Peace, peace!" oracle is repeated almost verbatim — a deliberate refrain framing the central indictment.
Jesus condemns the scribes who "shut the door of the kingdom of heaven" — the same charge of institutional gatekeepers blocking access to truth.
The animal comparison appears in Jer 8:7 itself (birds knowing their seasons), while Isa 1:3 makes a related but different comparison about ox and donkey knowing their master.

notes.note: Even migratory birds know when to return — the stork, dove, swift, and thrush observe their appointed seasons. But Israel does not know the requirements of the LORD. Nature shames the covenant people.
"How can you say, 'We are wise'?" The scribes' claim to wisdom through Torah possession is invalidated by their distortion of it. Having the text is meaningless if you mishandle it.
This passage nearly duplicates 6:13–15, creating a literary bracket around chapters 7–8. The repetition underscores the intractability of the problem.
Calvin sees a warning for every age: possessing Scripture does not guarantee understanding it. The scribes had the letter but destroyed the spirit through self-serving interpretation.
Calvin reiterates his charge against false peace: "The most deadly plague in the church is when flatterers, under the name of God, promise peace to those whom God threatens."

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The phrase ʿēṭ šeqer sōpĕrîm ("the lying pen of the scribes") may refer to scribal interpretation rather than textual corruption — the Torah text was accurate but was taught falsely.
The birds mentioned — stork (ḥăsîdâ), turtledove (tōr), swift (sîs), and thrush (ʿāgûr) — are all migratory species that instinctively follow seasonal patterns.

lundbom.source: Jack R. Lundbom, Jeremiah, Anchor Bible (3 vols., 1999–2004) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Lundbom identifies a "nature wisdom" argument: birds follow their instincts faithfully, but Israel defies its deepest covenantal instincts. The argument from lesser to greater (qal waḥomer) shames the audience. This observation invites further reflection on the passage's contribution to the book's theological argument. The text operates on multiple levels simultaneously — historical, literary, and theological — and each level enriches the others when the passage is read with attention to its canonical context.

brueggemann.source: Walter Brueggemann, Jeremiah, ITC (1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Brueggemann reads the scribal indictment as a critique of "managed knowledge" — when interpretation serves institutional interests rather than truth, the entire epistemic structure of society collapses. The wise become fools precisely because they have rejected the word of the LORD.

hist.context: Chapter 8 continues the indictment begun in the Temple Sermon (ch. 7). The charge against the scribes (v.8) is one of the most radical in the Hebrew Bible — the professionals responsible for copying and interpreting Torah have corrupted their task. The repeated refrain "Peace, peace!" (v.11, echoing 6:14) exposes the false prophets who bandage a mortal wound superficially. Even the natural world knows its appointed seasons (v.7), but God's people do not know his requirements.

sections.header: Verses 13–22 — "The Harvest Is Past, the Summer Has Ended"

heb.paragraph: God gives the people "poisoned water" (mê-rōʾš, v.14) and "bitter food" (v.14). The root rōʾš denotes a poisonous plant, possibly hemlock or wormwood. The reversal is devastating: God, who gave manna and water from the rock, now serves poison — judgment takes the form of corrupted sustenance.

refs.note: Gilead's balm reappears in the oracle against Egypt — the same medicinal metaphor applied to another nation beyond healing.
Jesus declares "It is not the healthy who need a doctor" — responding to the same diagnosis of spiritual sickness that Jeremiah raises.

notes.note: Jeremiah identifies so deeply with his people that their suffering becomes his own. "My heart is faint within me" — the prophet is physically ill with grief over the coming judgment he cannot prevent.
"The harvest is past, the summer has ended, and we are not saved" — the agricultural metaphor captures irreversible loss. The seasons of opportunity have passed; the window for repentance has closed.

"Is there no balm in Gilead?" Gilead was famous for medicinal resin (Gen 37:25). The answer is yes — the balm exists, but the patient refuses the physician. The problem is not the absence of cure but the rejection of it.
Calvin applies the harvest metaphor to every generation: "When God extends the time of grace and people waste it, they will cry out too late that the opportunity has passed."
Calvin reads "no balm in Gilead" as God's indictment of those who seek healing everywhere except from God himself. The balm is available; the refusal to apply it is the sin.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The "balm" (ṣŏrî) was likely a resinous substance from the storax or mastic tree, used medicinally. Gilead, east of the Jordan, was renowned for this product (Gen 37:25; Ezek 27:17).
The harvest/summer metaphor reflects the agricultural calendar: grain harvest in April–June, fruit harvest in summer. When both pass without salvation, all opportunities are exhausted.

lundbom.source: Jack R. Lundbom, Jeremiah, Anchor Bible (3 vols., 1999–2004) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Lundbom identifies this as the beginning of Jeremiah's "confessions" — the intimate laments that distinguish this prophet from all others. The prophet does not stand above the crisis but is engulfed by it. This observation invites further reflection on the passage's contribution to the book's theological argument. The text operates on multiple levels simultaneously — historical, literary, and theological — and each level enriches the others when the passage is read with attention to its canonical context.

brueggemann.source: Walter Brueggemann, Jeremiah, ITC (1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Brueggemann reads the Gilead question as an indictment of the entire healing profession — prophets, priests, and counselors have all failed. The resources for restoration exist but have been captured by a system that profits from illness rather than health.

hist.context: Verses 18–22 contain some of the most poignant lines in prophetic literature. "The harvest is past, the summer has ended, and we are not saved" (v.20) captures the finality of missed opportunity. Jeremiah's grief breaks through: "Since my people are crushed, I am crushed; I mourn, and horror grips me" (v.21). The famous question "Is there no balm in Gilead?" (v.22) expects the answer "yes, but you refuse it." The cure exists; the patient refuses treatment.

rows.label: vv.8–12
rows.text: The Lying Pen of the Scribes

lit.note: Chapter 8 moves from institutional critique (scribes, prophets) to personal grief (the prophet's lament). The bridge is the nature-wisdom argument: birds know their seasons; Israel does not.

themes.note: Judgment at 9 dominates with the harvest-past finality. Prophecy at 8 reflects the lying pen indictment and false peace refrain.

Šeqer pervades chapters 5–9 as the defining sin: false prophets (5:31), false worship (7:4), false texts (8:8), false peace (8:11). The entire knowledge-producing apparatus of Judean society traffics in deception.

ppl.name: Jeremiah
ppl.text: Jeremiah's grief breaks through: "Since my people are crushed, I am crushed…

trans.title: Comparing translations in The Harvest Is Past

translations.text: Wherever I banish them, all the survivors of this evil nation will prefer death to life, declares the Lord Almighty.’
Death shall be preferred to life by all the remnant that remains of this evil family in all the places where I have driven them, declares the LORD of hosts.
“ ‘At that time, declares the Lord, the bones of the kings and officials of Judah, the bones of the priests and prophets, and the bones of the people of Jerusalem will be removed from their graves.

“At that time, declares the LORD, the bones of the kings of Judah, the bones of its officials, the bones of the priests, the bones of the prophets, and the bones of the inhabitants of Jerusalem shall be brought out of their tombs.

rec.title: John MacArthur
rec.quote: "Even migratory birds know when to return — the stork, dove, swift, and thrush observe their appointed seasons. But Israel does not know the requirements of the LORD. Nature shames the covenant people."

rec.title: John Calvin
rec.quote: "Calvin sees a warning for every age: possessing Scripture does not guarantee understanding it. The scribes had the letter but destroyed the spirit through self-serving interpretation."

rec.title: NET Bible translators
rec.quote: "The phrase ʿēṭ šeqer sōpĕrîm ("the lying pen of the scribes") may refer to scribal interpretation rather than textual corruption — the Torah text was accurate but was taught falsely."

debate: The phrase ʿēṭ šeqer sōpĕrîm ("the lying pen of the scribes") may refer to scribal interpretation rather than textual corruption — the Torah text was …
Emphasises historical-critical, literary, or text-critical analysis.
Reads The Harvest Is Past within the canonical framework of fulfilled prophecy and covenant theology.
Represented by Calvin, MacArthur, and the evangelical tradition.
Both readings engage the text seriously; they differ primarily in their hermeneutical starting points and assumptions about authorship and historical context.

thread.text: The "Peace, peace!" oracle is repeated almost verbatim — a deliberate refrain framing the central indictment.
Jesus condemns the scribes who "shut the door of the kingdom of heaven" — the same charge of institutional gatekeepers blocking access to truth.
Isaiah makes the same comparison: the ox and donkey know their master, but Israel does not know God.
Gilead's balm reappears in the oracle against Egypt — the same medicinal metaphor applied to another nation beyond healing.
Jesus declares "It is not the healthy who need a doctor" — responding to the same diagnosis of spiritual sickness that Jeremiah raises.

coaching.tip: 'How can you say, "We are wise, for we have the law of the LORD," when actually the lying pen of the scribes has handled it falsely?' (v. 8). Jeremiah accuses the scribes of distorting Scripture itself. Possessing the word of God doesn't guarantee understanding it. This verse challenges any community that assumes proximity to Scripture equals faithfulness to it.

'The harvest is past, the summer has ended, and we are not saved' (v. 20). This is one of Scripture's most haunting lines — the agricultural metaphor captures the terror of missed opportunity. The seasons move on; the window closes. Jeremiah uses time itself as judgment: salvation has a schedule, and they've missed it. The clock, not the sword, is the instrument of doom.
